## [00:00 - 03:03]

That looks fishy. So we're going to go and do what we do with most sole practitioners. We're going to go ahead and start an audit. First and foremost, you lose the $10,000 expenses. If the IRS is right, which they are 90% of the time and I'll explain why they are in a second, they go in there and they win the audit, you lose the deduction. So now you have to pay whatever tax bracket you are at that $10,000 rate. Say you're at a 20% tax bracket. Well $2,000 of that goes to taxes. Then they're going to say, oh, you took a deduction that you shouldn't have. So we're going to put a penalty on that, which is going to be 10%. So that's another $1,000. And then we're going to charge you interest on that $3,000 until you pay it off. So then you're getting charged interest on that for the time being. Plus, you don't know how to do the repair of the tax returns. The last tidbit of having this, this kind of is an overall thing. Ask for an extension. What's an extension? Well, every year on April 15th, you've got to file your taxes and you got to pay for your taxes, but you can go to the IRS and say, listen, I'm not ready to file my full return yet. I'm going to pay my taxes on April 15th. I'm giving you notification, but my final return isn't going to be due until October 15th at the end of the year. Oh, okay. Well, what? How does that help you? Well, the IRS is already way behind. I don't want them to audit me in the first place. Instead of giving them almost a whole year to look at me, maybe I give them a shorter window period of time because if I file an October 15th, I've shortened that quite a bit.
